The surgical team prepares for a robotic retroperitoneal lymph node dissection on a 33-year-old male patient with a residual mass in the left renal hilum. The patient is positioned supine with arms tucked and legs straight, in a 20-degree Trendelenburg position. The surgical setup includes a four-port configuration with one assistant port, positioned below the umbilicus and projecting upwards towards the patient's head. The camera, equipped with a 30-degree lens, is positioned in the right lower quadrant, alongside robotic arms equipped with hot shears and a ProGrasp instrument.

The surgeon begins by creating an incision from the cecum to the ligament of Treitz, cutting through the posterior peritoneum. The cut edges of the posterior peritoneum are sutured to the abdominal wall to maintain exposure and keep the small bowel out of the surgical field.

This approach, supine, going from the bottom up, gives you full access. And I'll show you that. I don't think you could go from the top down because you'll have the bowel in your way. And so there is this window that if you can move the bowel cephalad to get everything out of your way. And that's what we're going to do.

The surgeon identifies the right iliac artery, ureter, and appendix, then makes an incision underneath the cecum through the posterior peritoneum. A flap is created and suspended to enhance exposure of the surgical field.

The surgeon uses bipolar forceps for blunt dissection, preferring them over the sharper Maryland dissector. A flap is being created with deliberate thickness, revealing the bowel and bowel mesentery. A lymph node is visible within the mesentery, but it is not the target of concern.

The surgeon moves to the left side, identifying a cut edge and postganglionic nerve fibers from the hypogastric plexus. The left common iliac artery is visible, and the lateral limit is determined to be the ureter. The surgeon examines a structure that may be the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA).
The surgeon uses two needle drivers to position a long suspension suture, a 3-0 monofilament suture with an SH needle, to elevate the right cut edge of the posterior peritoneum.

The surgeon adjusts the position to optimize exposure, ensuring the small bowel remains stable and does not fall into the surgical field. A Hemolock is used to secure structures, and the cecum is left intact to prevent the small bowel from shifting underneath.

The surgeon continues the dissection cephalad, identifying the duodenum and a blue structure, likely the left renal vein or possibly the inferior vena cava. The surgeon prepares to take the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) for better exposure due to a left-sided tumor, using scissors and bipolar forceps.

The surgeon identifies and defines anatomical structures including the left renal vein, ureter, and the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) beneath the left mesocolon. Large lymphatic channels originating from the pancreas are visible, crossing over the left renal vein, and are prepared for clipping.

The surgeon uses a robotic clip applier to secure a large lymphatic channel over the left renal vein, preventing potential chylous ascites. Multiple clips are applied to ensure closure of the lymphatic vessels, with adjustments made for optimal exposure and angle.

The surgeon identifies and manipulates lymphatic structures beneath the aorta, noting the presence of the gonadal vein and lumbar vein. The renal artery is visible, and needle drivers are prepared for suturing.
